Rational Design of DNA Probes for Biological Research and Clinical Testing

Summary: The applicant decoded the temporal structure of single-molecule mechanical signals in living cells; achieved rare-mutation detection in clinical samples; and designed DNA probes to solve biological problems in multiple fields.

One of the difficulties in single-molecule manipulation is to design and prepare DNA and RNA molecules that can be attached to surfaces and have special topological conformations according to specific needs. The applicant developed a general method for preparing complex DNA and RNA molecular probes, which was used to prepare DNA/RNA probes with various conformations for single-molecule manipulation (ACS Synth Biol 2019, sole corresponding author), laying the foundation for subsequent studies.

Cell mechanics is an important interdisciplinary field between cell biology and biophysics. When cells interact with the extracellular matrix or neighboring cells, they convert mechanical signals into intracellular biochemical signals, thereby regulating life processes such as cell adhesion, migration, proliferation, and differentiation. At present, multiple experimental methods can be used to measure the magnitude of mechanical signals. The loading rate and duration of mechanical signals determine the recognition and decoding by downstream proteins, but measuring them remains difficult; the bottleneck lies in: based on a deep understanding of the mechanical stability and force-bearing modes of DNA, designing multi-module mechanical molecular probes. We designed and calibrated DNA mechanical fluorescence probes and measured, at the single-molecule level, the dynamic maps of the magnitude, loading rate, and duration of cellular mechanical signals. The probe contains two DNA hairpin structures with specific mechanical thresholds and produces fluorescence through different fluorescent labels when the structures open, thereby sequentially recording the events of “low-threshold hairpin opening” and “high-threshold hairpin opening”. By calculating the interval between these two time points, the average loading rate of the force and the duration of the tensile force can be accurately determined. Using this DNA mechanical probe, we successfully captured the dynamic process of integrin mechanical signals evolving over time at the single-molecule level, and revealed the coupling rules among the magnitude, loading rate, and duration of mechanical signals in living cells, as well as the regulatory rules of cytoskeletal proteins and the microenvironment. This study laid a methodological foundation for exploring the roles of mechanical signals in immune recognition, tumor metastasis, and stem-cell differentiation (Fig. 6, representative work, Cell 2024, co-corresponding author).

Figure 6 Dynamic maps of single-molecule mechanical signals in living cells measured with DNA probes (Cell 2024, co-corresponding author, representative work). (A) The DNA probe contains two domains with different opening forces, and single-molecule magnetic tweezers can measure the opening force of each domain. (B) In single-molecule magnetic tweezers experiments, two opening signals of the mechanical probe can be observed at 17 pN and 41 pN, respectively; after the force is decreased, the probe refolds and closes. (C) In cell experiments, the loading rate and duration of mechanical signals can be measured by fluorescence.

In clinical diagnosis, quantitative detection of nucleic acids and mutation discrimination are of great significance. For example, miRNA expression profiles are closely related to multiple cancers and cardiovascular diseases, and timely and accurate detection of their expression levels can provide key references for early diagnosis and treatment monitoring. Genetic variations in RNA viruses directly affect transmissibility and immune escape ability, so accurate typing and identification of different strains and key mutation sites are required. However, most commonly used nucleic acid detection methods rely on amplification, which not only makes them susceptible to cross-contamination by amplification products, but also imposes higher requirements on experimental equipment and operating procedures. To meet this challenge, we used a single-molecule magnetic tweezers platform and designed tandem DNA hairpin probes for mechanical manipulation, realizing a detection method with high sensitivity, mutation discrimination, multiplex parallel detection, and reusable probes. Based on this method, we successfully identified rare miRNA mutations in serum (at the 10^-6 level), quantitatively analyzed miRNAs in liver cancer and adjacent tissues, accurately distinguished viral-strain mutations in saliva, and detected alleles in whole blood (all samples were from the Department of Laboratory Medicine of Renmin Hospital of Hubei Province) (Fig. 7, representative work, Nucleic Acids Res 2023, last corresponding author).

Figure 7 Manipulating tandem DNA hairpin probes to detect clinical nucleic acid samples (Nucleic Acids Res 2023, last corresponding author, representative work). (A) The DNA hairpin contains a specific sequence. When the hairpin is opened by force, the sample to be tested binds to the hairpin sequence and thereby blocks hairpin annealing. A test sample containing a mutation cannot effectively block hairpin annealing. (B) Connecting thousands of DNA hairpins in tandem can increase detection sensitivity by six orders of magnitude. (C) Multiple probes are used to detect multiple samples simultaneously.

To achieve high throughput, high concentrations of DNA probes must be used. However, high concentrations of DNA probes lead to multiple DNA molecules simultaneously attached to the same bead (multiple molecules) and DNA molecules attached to the surface by only one end (surface-interfering molecules), which generate false signals and create a dilemma. For application to clinical sample testing rather than remaining only in the laboratory, an urgent challenge is to avoid false signals while ensuring high throughput. To overcome this technical challenge, the applicant applied a force of approximately 80 pN, causing DNA to undergo a phase transition and form S-DNA. Because S-DNA is not cleaved by endonucleases, endonucleases can automatically remove multiple molecules and surface-interfering molecules. This method increased the experimental throughput to approximately 1000 molecules while ensuring high accuracy, solving the dilemma between high throughput and accuracy (Fig. 8, ACS Sensors 2024, sole corresponding author).

Figure 8 Removal of erroneous interfering DNA molecules in high-throughput single-molecule experiments (ACS Sensors 2024, sole corresponding author). (A) A self-designed and self-built temperature-controlled high-throughput magnetic tweezers instrument. (B) A force of 80 pN converts correctly tethered DNA into S-DNA, which is not enzymatically cleaved, while endonucleases remove multiple molecules and surface-interfering molecules.

In addition to the applications of the DNA probes described above, we also developed new methods and designed and synthesized single-stranded DNA probes, finding that Rtt105 can directly promote the assembly of RPA protein on single-stranded DNA, thereby improving the efficiency of homologous recombination DNA repair (PNAS 2021, co-corresponding author). In the field of gene editing technology, we constructed single-base-mutation DNA probes and used differences in mechanical stability to explain the low off-target effect of Cas12a mutants in gene editing (PloS Biol 2024, co-corresponding author). In DNA sequencing, we designed a glass-microneedle-based DNA probing technology, which is expected to be developed into a solid-state nanopore sequencing method (ACS Nano 2024, co-corresponding author). To solve the bottleneck in research on biomolecular sugars, we also designed probes and entered the blank field of single-molecule manipulation of sugars (Carbohydr Polym 2025, last corresponding author).

Multiple achievements related to DNA mechanical probes have enabled the applicant, starting from the study of the fundamental physical properties of DNA and RNA, to deeply integrate with biological research such as cell mechanics and with clinical detection applications.
